title: The River and the Stone
content:
In a valley between two green hills, a wide and ancient river ran past a small village. The river had been flowing for thousands of years. Over that time, it had carved a deep canyon through solid rock so beautiful that travelers came from distant lands just to see it.

In the village lived a boy named Theo who struggled greatly in school. Reading was difficult. Numbers tumbled around in his head and refused to stay in place. Other children seemed to learn things easily, but Theo had to try three times harder just to keep up.

One afternoon, Theo sat on the river bank feeling very discouraged after failing a test. He stared at a large, smooth boulder in the middle of the river. The water rushed against it, divided in two, and flowed neatly around both sides without stopping.

He watched the water for a long time. It never gave up. When the boulder was too big to go over, the river went around. Where there was a tiny crack in the rock, the water found it and worked its way through, patiently, widening it year by year.

He thought about the deep canyon downstream. Stone walls rose hundreds of feet on either side. Water had carved all of that, not by smashing through in one mighty blow, but by flowing every day, every season, for thousands of years without stopping.

An old fisherman came and sat beside Theo. "Do you know why the river can cut through stone?" he asked. Theo shook his head. "It is not because the river is powerful," said the fisherman. "It is because the river never gives up. It simply keeps going, always."

Theo walked home slowly, thinking. That evening he sat at his desk and opened his book again. He did not try to learn everything at once. He worked on one small piece, then another. When something was too hard, he went around it and came back later.

Weeks passed and then months. Slowly, things began to make more sense. He still had hard days. But on those days he remembered the river, quiet and persistent and unstoppable, and he remembered that the canyon was not carved in a single day. He kept going.
